message:
feat(session): session management improvements

- lib.rs: enhanced session handling

Syncs beads tracker.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-x1x","title":"Integrate prepared statement protocol into MySqlAsyncConnection","description":"The prepared statement binary protocol is implemented in protocol/prepared.rs (bd-1a4 complete), but the MySqlAsyncConnection methods (prepare, query_prepared, execute_prepared) are currently stubs.

## Implementation Needed

1. **prepare()**: Send COM_STMT_PREPARE, parse response, return PreparedStatement
2. **query_prepared()**: Send COM_STMT_EXECUTE, parse binary result set
3. **execute_prepared()**: Same as query_prepared but return affected rows
4. **Close statements**: Track open statement IDs, send COM_STMT_CLOSE on connection close

SharedMySqlConnection Connection trait now delegates to these methods.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-x1x","depends_on_id":"sqlmodel_rust-0gv","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T17:18:02.409034804Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-y77","title":"Convert sqlmodel-mysql to async Connection trait","description":"The MySQL driver has complete wire protocol implementation but is synchronous only. Migration needed:
1. Replace std::net::TcpStream with asupersync::net::TcpStream
2. Convert read_packet/write_packet to async with .await
3. Add &Cx context parameter to all async methods
4. Return Outcome<T, E> instead of Result<T, E>
5. Add cancellation checks via cx.checkpoint()

This enables MySQL query execution with the rest of the stack (Session, Pool, etc).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:24:18.036923422Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:25:13.263648906Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T02:25:13.263586199Z","close_reason":"Already implemented - MySqlAsyncConnection and SharedMySqlConnection both implement Connection trait","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-zhp","title":"Implement Theme struct with SQLModel color palette","description":"# Implement Theme Struct with SQLModel Color Palette

## Task Description

Create a Theme struct that defines the color palette used throughout sqlmodel_rust
console output. This ensures visual consistency across all renderables.

## File: src/theme.rs

```rust
//! Theme definitions for SQLModel console output.
//!
//! Provides a consistent color palette for all styled output.

#[cfg(feature = "rich")]
use rich_rust::style::{Color, Style};

/// SQLModel console theme with semantic colors.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct Theme {
    // === Status Colors ===
    /// Success messages, completion indicators
    pub success: ThemeColor,
    /// Error messages, failure indicators  
    pub error: ThemeColor,
    /// Warning messages, deprecation notices
    pub warning: ThemeColor,
    /// Informational messages, hints
    pub info: ThemeColor,
    
    // === SQL Value Type Colors ===
    /// NULL values (typically dim/italic)
    pub null_value: ThemeColor,
    /// Boolean values (true/false)
    pub bool_value: ThemeColor,
    /// Numeric values (integers, floats)
    pub number_value: ThemeColor,
    /// String/text values
    pub string_value: ThemeColor,
    /// Date/time/timestamp values
    pub date_value: ThemeColor,
    /// Binary/blob values
    pub binary_value: ThemeColor,
    /// JSON values
    pub json_value: ThemeColor,
    /// UUID values
    pub uuid_value: ThemeColor,
    
    // === SQL Syntax Colors ===
    /// SQL keywords (SELECT, FROM, WHERE)
    pub sql_keyword: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL strings ('value')
    pub sql_string: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL numbers (42, 3.14)
    pub sql_number: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL comments (-- comment)
    pub sql_comment: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL operators (=, >, AND)
    pub sql_operator: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL identifiers (table names, column names)
    pub sql_identifier: ThemeColor,
    
    // === UI Element Colors ===
    /// Table/panel borders
    pub border: ThemeColor,
    /// Headers and titles
    pub header: ThemeColor,
    /// Dimmed/secondary text
    pub dim: ThemeColor,
    /// Highlighted/emphasized text
    pub highlight: ThemeColor,
}

/// A color that can be rendered differently based on mode.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct ThemeColor {
    /// RGB color for rich mode (r, g, b)
    pub rgb: (u8, u8, u8),
    /// ANSI 256-color fallback
    pub ansi256: u8,
    /// Plain text marker (for plain mode output)
    pub plain_marker: Option<&'static str>,
}

impl ThemeColor {
    /// Create a theme color with RGB and ANSI256 fallback.
    pub const fn new(rgb: (u8, u8, u8), ansi256: u8) -> Self {
        Self { rgb, ansi256, plain_marker: None }
    }
    
    /// Create with a plain text marker for non-color output.
    pub const fn with_marker(rgb: (u8, u8, u8), ansi256: u8, marker: &'static str) -> Self {
        Self { rgb, ansi256, plain_marker: Some(marker) }
    }
    
    /// Convert to rich_rust Color (truecolor).
    #[cfg(feature = "rich")]
    pub fn to_color(&self) -> Color {
        Color::from_rgb(self.rgb.0, self.rgb.1, self.rgb.2)
    }
    
    /// Convert to rich_rust Style with this as foreground.
    #[cfg(feature = "rich")]
    pub fn to_style(&self) -> Style {
        Style::new().color(self.to_color())
    }
}

impl Theme {
    /// Create the default dark theme (Dracula-inspired).
    pub fn dark() -> Self {
        Self {
            // Status colors (Dracula palette)
            success: ThemeColor::new((80, 250, 123), 84),      // Green
            error: ThemeColor::new((255, 85, 85), 203),        // Red
            warning: ThemeColor::new((241, 250, 140), 228),    // Yellow
            info: ThemeColor::new((139, 233, 253), 117),       // Cyan
            
            // Value type colors
            null_value: ThemeColor::with_marker((98, 114, 164), 60, "NULL"),
            bool_value: ThemeColor::new((241, 250, 140), 228),  // Yellow
            number_value: ThemeColor::new((139, 233, 253), 117), // Cyan
            string_value: ThemeColor::new((80, 250, 123), 84),   // Green
            date_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 121, 198), 212),   // Magenta
            binary_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 184, 108), 215), // Orange
            json_value: ThemeColor::new((189, 147, 249), 141),   // Purple
            uuid_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 184, 108), 215),   // Orange
            
            // SQL syntax colors
            sql_keyword: ThemeColor::new((255, 121, 198), 212),  // Magenta
            sql_string: ThemeColor::new((80, 250, 123), 84),     // Green
            sql_number: ThemeColor::new((189, 147, 249), 141),   // Purple
            sql_comment: ThemeColor::new((98, 114, 164), 60),    // Gray
            sql_operator: ThemeColor::new((255, 85, 85), 203),   // Red
            sql_identifier: ThemeColor::new((248, 248, 242), 255), // White
            
            // UI elements
            border: ThemeColor::new((98, 114, 164), 60),        // Gray
            header: ThemeColor::new((248, 248, 242), 255),      // White
            dim: ThemeColor::new((98, 114, 164), 60),           // Gray
            highlight: ThemeColor::new((255, 255, 255), 231),   // Bright white
        }
    }
    
    /// Create a light theme variant.
    pub fn light() -> Self {
        Self {
            // Status colors (adjusted for light background)
            success: ThemeColor::new((40, 167, 69), 34),
            error: ThemeColor::new((220, 53, 69), 160),
            warning: ThemeColor::new((255, 193, 7), 220),
            info: ThemeColor::new((23, 162, 184), 37),
            
            // Value colors (darker for visibility on light bg)
            null_value: ThemeColor::with_marker((108, 117, 125), 244, "NULL"),
            bool_value: ThemeColor::new((156, 39, 176), 128),
            number_value: ThemeColor::new((0, 150, 136), 30),
            string_value: ThemeColor::new((76, 175, 80), 34),
            date_value: ThemeColor::new((156, 39, 176), 128),
            binary_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 152, 0), 208),
            json_value: ThemeColor::new((103, 58, 183), 92),
            uuid_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 152, 0), 208),
            
            // SQL syntax (darker)
            sql_keyword: ThemeColor::new((156, 39, 176), 128),
            sql_string: ThemeColor::new((76, 175, 80), 34),
            sql_number: ThemeColor::new((103, 58, 183), 92),
            sql_comment: ThemeColor::new((108, 117, 125), 244),
            sql_operator: ThemeColor::new((220, 53, 69), 160),
            sql_identifier: ThemeColor::new((33, 37, 41), 235),
            
            // UI elements
            border: ThemeColor::new((108, 117, 125), 244),
            header: ThemeColor::new((33, 37, 41), 235),
            dim: ThemeColor::new((108, 117, 125), 244),
            highlight: ThemeColor::new((0, 0, 0), 16),
        }
    }
}

impl Default for Theme {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self::dark()
    }
}
```

## Color Philosophy

### Dracula Palette Base
The dark theme uses colors from the Dracula palette because:
1. It's widely recognized and loved by developers
2. High contrast ratios for accessibility
3. Distinct colors for each semantic meaning
4. Looks professional in terminal screenshots

### Semantic Consistency
- **Green** = Success, strings (positive/data)
- **Red** = Errors, operators (danger/action)
- **Yellow** = Warnings, booleans (caution/special)
- **Cyan** = Info, numbers (neutral data)
- **Magenta** = Dates, SQL keywords (special syntax)
- **Purple** = JSON, SQL numbers (structured data)
- **Gray** = Dim text, comments, borders (secondary)

### Accessibility
- All colors chosen to meet WCAG contrast guidelines
- ANSI256 fallbacks for terminals without truecolor
- Plain markers for complete accessibility

## Verification

```bash
cargo check -p sqlmodel-console --features rich
cargo test -p sqlmodel-console theme::tests
```","acceptance_criteria":"Theme struct has dark and light variants
ThemeColor enum supports RGB, ANSI256, and basic colors
Theme provides semantic colors for all SQL elements
Theme::from_env() detects user preference
All unit tests pass for theme functionality
Theme colors render correctly in both Rich and Plain modes","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:05:02.295059680Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-21T09:12:31.070100671Z","closed_at":"2026-01-21T09:12:31.069985544Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["phase-2","rich-rust","theme"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-zhp","depends_on_id":"bd-1ob","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:53:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-zhp","depends_on_id":"bd-25i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:53:31Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":34,"issue_id":"bd-zhp","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Required Unit Tests

1. test_dark_theme_creation - verify Theme::dark() returns valid theme
2. test_light_theme_creation - verify Theme::light() returns valid theme
3. test_default_theme_is_dark - verify Default trait uses dark theme
4. test_theme_color_rgb_values - verify RGB values are valid (0-255)
5. test_theme_color_ansi256_values - verify ANSI256 values are valid (0-255)
6. test_theme_color_to_style - verify ThemeColor::to_style() creates valid Style
7. test_status_colors_distinct - verify success/error/warning/info are distinguishable
8. test_sql_value_colors_complete - verify all SQL types have colors
9. test_sql_syntax_colors_complete - verify all SQL syntax elements have colors
10. test_theme_color_with_marker - verify plain_marker field works","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:35:39Z"}]}
-{"id":"bd-zy3","title":"Implement Session.is_modified() for dirty checking","description":"## Description

Check if an object has pending changes.

## Python Behavior

```python
user.name = 'New Name'
session.is_modified(user)  # True

session.is_modified(user, include_collections=False)  # Check attrs only
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
impl Session {
    pub fn is_modified<M: Model>(&self, model: &M) -> bool {
        let pk = model.primary_key_value();
        if let Some(state) = self.get_state::<M>(&pk) {
            return state.is_dirty();
        }
        false
    }
    
    pub fn modified_attributes<M: Model>(&self, model: &M) -> Vec<&str> {
        // Return list of changed attribute names
    }
}
```

## Dirty Tracking

Need to track original values:
```rust
struct ObjectState {
    original_values: HashMap<String, Value>,
    pending: ObjectPendingState,
}

enum ObjectPendingState {
    New,
    Clean,
    Dirty { changed: HashSet<String> },
    Deleted,
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Returns true if object has unsaved changes
- [ ] Returns false for clean objects
- [ ] Works with new (pending) objects
- [ ] Can check collections separately
- [ ] modified_attributes() returns changed field names

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/session/dirty.rs)
- [ ] Test is_modified for unchanged object
- [ ] Test is_modified for changed field
- [ ] Test is_modified for relationship change
- [ ] Test is_modified for new object
- [ ] Test is_modified for deleted object

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/session_dirty.rs)
- [ ] Load object → is_modified=false
- [ ] Change field → is_modified=true
- [ ] Flush → is_modified=false again
- [ ] is_modified with nested objects
- [ ] Specific field dirty check

### Logging
- [ ] TRACE: Dirty field detection
- [ ] DEBUG: Dirty state changes

 {"id":"sqlmodel_rust-0gv","title":"SQLModel Rust: MySQL Protocol Implementation","description":"# Epic: MySQL Protocol Implementation (sqlmodel-mysql)

## Overview
This epic implements the MySQL wire protocol from scratch. MySQL uses a different protocol than PostgreSQL but follows similar principles: TCP connection, length-prefixed packets, and request-response patterns.

## Rationale
Same reasoning as PostgreSQL: full control, native async, cancellation support, and first-principles understanding. MySQL is important for:
- Huge existing deployments
- AWS Aurora MySQL
- MariaDB compatibility
- Different feature set than PostgreSQL

## Key Components

### 1. Packet Protocol (protocol.rs)
MySQL uses 4-byte header packets:
- 3 bytes: payload length
- 1 byte: sequence number
- Payload

Packets over 16MB are split. Sequence numbers track request/response pairing.

### 2. Connection Handshake (handshake.rs)
Initial handshake:
1. Server sends HandshakeV10:
   - Protocol version
   - Server version string
   - Connection ID
   - Auth plugin data (salt)
   - Capability flags
   - Character set
   - Status flags
2. Client sends HandshakeResponse:
   - Capability flags
   - Max packet size
   - Character set
   - Username, auth response
   - Database name
   - Auth plugin name

### 3. Authentication (auth.rs)
MySQL auth plugins:
- mysql_native_password: SHA1-based (legacy)
- caching_sha2_password: SHA256-based (default MySQL 8.0+)
- sha256_password: RSA-based
- auth_gssapi_client: Kerberos

caching_sha2_password flow:
1. Client sends scrambled password
2. Server may request full auth (public key exchange)
3. Client encrypts password with server's public key
4. Server verifies

### 4. Command Protocol (commands.rs)
MySQL commands (COM_xxx):
- COM_QUERY: Text protocol query
- COM_STMT_PREPARE: Prepare statement
- COM_STMT_EXECUTE: Execute prepared
- COM_STMT_CLOSE: Close prepared
- COM_STMT_RESET: Reset statement
- COM_PING: Keep-alive
- COM_QUIT: Close connection
- COM_INIT_DB: Change database
- COM_FIELD_LIST: Get columns (deprecated)

### 5. Result Sets (results.rs)
Text protocol results:
1. Column count packet
2. Column definition packets (per column)
3. EOF packet (or OK if no results)
4. Row packets (length-encoded strings)
5. EOF packet (end of rows)

Binary protocol results (prepared statements):
- Similar structure but binary encoding
- NULL bitmap for nullable columns
- Type-specific binary encoding

### 6. Type System (types.rs)
MySQL types to Rust:
- MYSQL_TYPE_TINY -> i8
- MYSQL_TYPE_SHORT -> i16
- MYSQL_TYPE_LONG -> i32
- MYSQL_TYPE_LONGLONG -> i64
- MYSQL_TYPE_FLOAT -> f32
- MYSQL_TYPE_DOUBLE -> f64
- MYSQL_TYPE_STRING, VARCHAR -> String
- MYSQL_TYPE_BLOB -> Vec<u8>
- MYSQL_TYPE_DATE -> date
- MYSQL_TYPE_DATETIME -> datetime
- MYSQL_TYPE_JSON -> serde_json::Value

Length-encoded integers:
- < 251: 1 byte
- < 2^16: 0xFC + 2 bytes
- < 2^24: 0xFD + 3 bytes
- < 2^64: 0xFE + 8 bytes

### 7. Prepared Statements (prepared.rs)
Binary protocol advantages:
- No SQL injection (parameters separate)
- Efficient binary encoding
- Server-side caching
- Correct type handling

Flow:
1. COM_STMT_PREPARE -> statement ID, param count, column count
2. COM_STMT_EXECUTE with binary parameters
3. Binary result set

### 8. Error Handling (error.rs)
ERR packet format:
- 0xFF marker
- Error code (2 bytes)
- SQL state marker '#'
- SQL state (5 bytes)
- Error message

Map MySQL error codes to meaningful errors.

### 9. Transactions
MySQL transaction commands:
- START TRANSACTION
- COMMIT, ROLLBACK
- SAVEPOINT, ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT
- SET autocommit = 0/1

### 10. Character Sets
MySQL character set handling:
- Connection charset
- Result charset
- Collation

Default to utf8mb4 for full Unicode.

## Key Design Decisions
1. **Async native**: Use asupersync TCP throughout
2. **Capability negotiation**: Support both old and new features
3. **Auth plugin system**: Extensible auth mechanism
4. **Prepared statement cache**: Reuse prepared statements
5. **Connection reset**: Use COM_RESET_CONNECTION vs reconnect

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Connect to MySQL 5.7 and 8.0+
- [ ] All auth methods work
- [ ] Text protocol queries
- [ ] Binary protocol prepared statements
- [ ] All common types map correctly
- [ ] Transactions work correctly
- [ ] Error codes mapped to Rust errors
- [ ] SSL/TLS connections
- [ ] Connection pooling integration
- [ ] MariaDB compatibility
- [ ] Correct character set handling

## Dependencies
- sqlmodel-core (Connection trait, Value, Row, Error)
- asupersync (TCP, cancellation, Budget)

## Estimated Scope
~2000 lines of protocol implementation

## References
- MySQL Protocol: https://dev.mysql.com/doc/dev/mysql-server/latest/PAGE_PROTOCOL.html
- MariaDB Protocol: https://mariadb.com/kb/en/clientserver-protocol/","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","assignee":"TealOtter","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T16:19:14.902857954Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-27T17:44:13.459380770Z","closed_at":"2026-01-27T17:44:13.459296042Z","close_reason":"All subtasks completed: Connection trait, async connection, prepared statements, transactions, integration tests, and TLS support","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"sqlmodel_rust-53e","title":"SQLModel Rust: Core Foundation Layer","description":"# Epic: Core Foundation Layer (sqlmodel-core)

## Overview
This epic covers the foundational types and traits that all other components build upon. The core layer defines the contract between the ORM layer and database drivers, providing type-safe abstractions for values, rows, connections, and errors.

## Rationale
In Python SQLModel, the core types are spread across Pydantic (for validation types) and SQLAlchemy (for database types). In Rust, we consolidate these into a single coherent type system that leverages:
- Rust's enum system for type-safe value representation
- Traits for connection abstraction (allowing multiple backends)
- asupersync's Outcome type for cancellation-aware operations

## Key Design Decisions
1. **Value enum over dynamic typing**: Unlike Python's runtime type coercion, we use a comprehensive Value enum that covers all SQL types with explicit conversions
2. **Row as indexed + named access**: Support both positional (index) and named (column) access patterns
3. **Connection trait with async**: All operations are async and take Cx for structured concurrency
4. **Error hierarchy**: Typed errors for different failure modes (connection, query, type conversion)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] All SQL primitive types representable in Value enum
- [ ] Bidirectional conversion between Rust types and Value
- [ ] Connection trait supports query, execute, transaction
- [ ] Full asupersync integration (Cx, Outcome, cancellation)
- [ ] Comprehensive error types with context
- [ ] Zero unsafe code in public API

## Dependencies
- asupersync crate (external)
- serde for serialization traits

## Estimated Scope
~1500 lines of core type definitions and trait implementations","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","assignee":"","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T16:16:51.159275429Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T08:54:32.757539001Z","closed_at":"2026-01-18T08:54:32.757539001Z","close_reason":"Epic complete: All 6 subtasks closed. Value enum, Row, Connection/Transaction traits, Error types, Model trait/FieldInfo, and unit tests are all implemented with comprehensive coverage.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"sqlmodel_rust-53e.1","title":"Core: Implement comprehensive Value enum","description":"# Task: Implement Comprehensive Value Enum

## Context
The Value enum is the universal representation for all SQL values in SQLModel Rust. It must support every SQL type we need while maintaining type safety and efficient conversion.

## Current State
Basic Value enum exists with: Null, Bool, TinyInt, SmallInt, Integer, BigInt, Real, Double, Text, Blob.

## Required Enhancements

### Additional Variants Needed
```rust
pub enum Value {
    // Existing
    Null,
    Bool(bool),
    TinyInt(i8),
    SmallInt(i16),
    Integer(i32),
    BigInt(i64),
    Real(f32),
    Double(f64),
    Text(String),
    Blob(Vec<u8>),
    
    // Add these
    Decimal(String),           // Exact decimal (stored as string for precision)
    Date(i32),                 // Days since Unix epoch
    Time(i64),                 // Microseconds since midnight
    DateTime(i64),             // Microseconds since Unix epoch
    Timestamp(i64),            // Same as DateTime but with timezone awareness flag
    TimestampTz(i64, i32),     // Timestamp + timezone offset seconds
    Interval(i64, i32, i32),   // Microseconds, days, months
    Uuid([u8; 16]),            // 128-bit UUID
    Json(String),              // JSON stored as string
    JsonB(Vec<u8>),            // Binary JSON (PostgreSQL)
    Array(Vec<Value>),         // Homogeneous array
    Point(f64, f64),           // 2D point (x, y)
    Inet(IpAddr),              // IP address
    MacAddr([u8; 6]),          // MAC address
}
```

### Conversion Traits
Implement From<T> for Value:
- From<bool>, From<i8>, From<i16>, From<i32>, From<i64>
- From<f32>, From<f64>
- From<String>, From<&str>
- From<Vec<u8>>, From<&[u8]>
- From<Option<T>> where T: Into<Value>
- From<chrono::NaiveDate>, From<chrono::NaiveDateTime> (feature-gated)
- From<uuid::Uuid> (feature-gated)

### TryFrom<Value> for types
- TryFrom<Value> for bool, i8, i16, i32, i64
- TryFrom<Value> for f32, f64
- TryFrom<Value> for String
- TryFrom<Value> for Vec<u8>
- TryFrom<Value> for Option<T>

### Helper Methods
```rust
impl Value {
    pub fn is_null(&self) -> bool;
    pub fn as_i64(&self) -> Option<i64>;
    pub fn as_f64(&self) -> Option<f64>;
    pub fn as_str(&self) -> Option<&str>;
    pub fn as_bytes(&self) -> Option<&[u8]>;
    pub fn type_name(&self) -> &'static str;
}
```

## Testing Requirements
- Round-trip test: T -> Value -> T for all types
- Null coercion: Value::Null to Option<T> == None
- Type mismatch: Correct error when converting incompatible types
- Edge cases: i64::MAX, f64::INFINITY, empty strings, empty blobs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All variants listed above are implemented
- [ ] From<T> implemented for all common Rust types
- [ ] TryFrom<Value> implemented with proper errors
- [ ] Helper methods work correctly
- [ ] 100% test coverage for conversions
- [ ] No panics in any conversion path

## Files to Modify
- crates/sqlmodel-core/src/value.rs
- crates/sqlmodel-core/src/types.rs (if SqlType needs updates)
